results.title: وصول رواد الفضاء للمركبة «شنتشو-21» إلى بكين
results.description: وصل طاقم مركبة الفضاء «شنتشو-21» إلى بكين اليوم السبت على متن طائرة بعد إنجاز مهمته بنجاح. وعاد رواد الفضاء الثلاثة: تشانغ لو، و وو في، وتشانغ هونغ تشانغ، إلى الأرض بسلام مساء يوم الجمعة الماضي على متن مركبة الفضاء المأهولة «شنتشو-22» مُنهين بذلك إقامتهم التي دامت لـ210 أيام في المدار. وأفادت وكالة الفضاء المأهول الصينية بأن جميع أفراد الطاقم في صحة جيدة، مشيرة إلى نجاح مهمة مركبة الفضاء «شنتشو-21» التام. وبينت أن الطاقم سيدخل إلى فترة من الحجر الصحي الطبي سيخضع خلالها لفحوصات طبية شاملة وتقييمات صحية.
creator: عكاظ

results.title: US deal with Tehran remains elusive, Pentagon threatens to restart war
results.description: US deal with Tehran remains elusive, Pentagon threatens to restart war “Our ability to recommence if necessary...we are more than capable,” Hegseth said in Singapore. Saturday 30/05/2026 US Secretary of Defence Pete Hegseth speaks at the IISS Shangri-La Dialogue security summit in Singapore, May 30, 2026. SINGAPORE The US president made no decision on a potential deal with Iran after a two-hour meeting in the Situation Room as gaps remained between the two countries’ positions while the Pentagon threatened to unleash a new wave of attacks on Iran if no deal can be reached. “President Trump will only make a deal that is good for America and satisfies his redlines. Iran can never possess a nuclear weapon,” a US official said. The US president also said Iran would have to end its chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz and dismantle its capacity to make a nuclear weapon, two conditions that Tehran has not agreed to. “Iran must agree that they will never have a Nuclear Weapon or Bomb. The Hormuz Strait must be immediately open, no tolls, for unrestricted shipping traffic, in both directions,” Trump said, adding that nuclear material would be “unearthed” by the US. Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei pushed back on Trump’s conditions, telling state media that the Islamic republic “said goodbye to the language of ‘must’ 47 years ago.” Exchanges of messages were continuing, he added, but “no final agreement has been reached yet.” Iran’s semi-official Fars news agency, citing sources, said Trump’s comments were an “attempt to portray a fabricated victory.” A senior Iranian source, speaking on condition of anonymity, said the potential deal does not include any nuclear-related issues, while Baghaei said on state TV that the management of the strait must be decided by Iran and Oman. Fars said the strait would be reopened under Tehran’s conditions after the US lifts its blockade on Iranian ships. US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the US blockade would be removed slowly if it happens. Fars cited sources as saying Tehran was also demanding “the immediate release of $12 billion in frozen Iranian assets” before moving to the next phase of negotiations. But no agreement was reached in this regard. On the toll-free reopening of Hormuz, the sources quoted by Fars said “no such clause appears in the text of the agreement,” while Trump’s comment on destroying Iran’s nuclear material “is fundamentally baseless.” Trump said no money would be exchanged “until further notice”, a possible reference to Iran’s demands for toll payments in the strait, war damage reparations or a release of frozen Iranian assets. Meanwhile, American Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth said on Saturday the US is ready to restart attacks on Iran if a deal cannot be reached. “Our ability to recommence if necessary...we are more than capable,” Hegseth said in Singapore. “Our stockpiles are more than suited for that, both there and around the globe, so we’re in a very good place,” he added. The Pentagon chief said President Donald Trump was “patient” and wants to make a “great deal” that ensures Iran does not get a nuclear weapon. Hegseth, speaking at the Shangri-La Dialogue, Asia’s premier forum for defence leaders, militaries and diplomats, said the U.S. has not turned its back on the Asia-Pacific region despite being engaged in conflict with Iran. “We can do two things at one time. We’re super-charging our defence industrial base so that we’re building 2X, 3X, 4X the munitions very soon to ensure that all of our (operations) plans are properly funded throughout the world,” he said. On Friday, Trump said he would meet in a secure White House room to make a “final determination” on a proposal to end the Iran war, which would extend an early-April truce for another 60 days, giving negotiators time to forge a permanent end to the conflict. He was however unable to reach a decision, according to US media reports. The war launched by the US and Israel on February 28 has killed thousands of people, mainly in Iran and Lebanon, and caused global economic pain by pushing up energy prices due to Iran’s effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The efforts to strike a deal mediated by Pakistan, were thrown into question this week by US strikes on the southern Iranian port of Bandar Abbas countered by retaliatory fire from Iran. Trump is under pressure at home to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and get US gasoline prices down ahead of the November congressional elections, as voters show increasing frustration over rising prices. At the same time, he faces a potential backlash from anti-Iran hawks in his own party over any concessions to Tehran. Suggested By Editor US-Iran deal would extend ceasefire, free Hormuz traffic but delay thorny issues Suggested By Editor US-Iran deal would extend ceasefire, free Hormuz traffic but delay thorny issues
